Unconventional binding of calmodulin to CHK2 kinase inhibits catalytic activity

evidence_entities.text: Calmodulin (CaM) serves an essential role in eukaryotic cells as a Ca2+ sensor. Ca2+ binding leads to conformation changes in CaM that enable engagement of a repertoire of enzymes and the regulation of their catalytic activities.
Here, we report an unexpected mechanism by which CaM can bind CHK2 kinase to inhibit its kinase activity.

Using biochemical, biophysical and structural mass spectrometry, we identify a direct interaction of Ca2+-CaM with the CHK2 kinase domain that suppresses CHK2 catalytic activity in vitro and identify K373 in CHK2 as crucial for cell proliferation in human cells following DNA damage.
Surprisingly, like CaMKK2, immobilised CHK2 similarly bound CaM in a Ca2+-dependent manner that was compromised by the Ca2+ chelator, EGTA.
Surprisingly, these assays revealed that CHK2 activity was attenuated ~10 fold by CaM.
These experiments supported a direct interaction between Ca2+-CaM and CHK2, with a low micromolar affinity (Kd = 21.6 ± 4.7 μM).
Using either cross-linker, interactions with CaM were confined to the CHK2 kinase domain, even though full length recombinant CHK2 was used in these studies.
K255A, K373A and K465A CHK2 exhibited diminished sensitivity to Ca2+-CaM inhibition.

Following CRISPR-knockin, analysis of cell proliferation using IncuCyte live cell imaging revealed that, while CHK2K373A RPE cells grew, they exhibited muted proliferation compared with wildtype RPE cells.
Cell proliferation of CHK2K373A RPE cells was significantly reduced in the presence of 10 nM and 100 nM CPT compared with wildtype RPE cells.
